title: Joe applies the 'decisions are emotional' principle to dismantle the 'how do I trust my intuition' question
source_path: transcripts/2022-07-08_How Do I Trust Myself? (Coaching Session)/transcript.md
context: A woman who has been doing intensive parts work arrives at Joe with what looks like an intellectual problem: 'how do I trust my intuition, given that I have many parts each claiming to be intuition?' Joe immediately reads the intellectual-question-with-emotional-body signal (turn 1 — Joe's first move says the question is intellectual and not personal). Then over the next ~30 turns Joe applies a coherent framework: he uses the principle 'decisions are emotional' and the move of pointing to the just-occurring action ('you just spoke — how did that happen?') to dismantle the deciding-vs-flowing duality. This is the cleanest framework-application excerpt in the corpus.
primary_demonstrates: methodology-fidelity (framework: decisions-are-emotional + signature reframes) + intervention-quality (using live moments as proof) + perceptual-accuracy (catching intellectual-question-with-emotional-body)

turns.content: It's funny, when I scheduled this with Sarah, I had a topic in mind, and now it's not alive for me anymore. So in the last few days, I started thinking about intuition. And this morning I was meditating and a few seconds before my meditation ended, I had a thought that popped up intuitively. So the idea is around emotional fluidity, about how does labeling our emotions affect the ability for them to transform and change? And how does labeling emotions, how do we put structure or identity on who we are, and how does that affect our transformation and change?

So I'm happy to answer those questions, but I noticed two things that are happening. First, that's a really intellectual question and not personal to you. And then the second thing is I noticed that your emotion — like, because you're here in person, I noticed that there's actually something personal happening as far as, like, your emotional body is not having an intellectual conversation with me, but whatever just happened was intellectual. So I'm wondering if you have something else you want to work on?
That's good. I feel like I've been trying to find this perfect question for you. No, I don't really know what that is.
What's the thing that if it's shifted in your life would be the most profound positive movement that you could conceive of?
Being able to trust myself again.
Okay, that sounds like something to work on.

Yeah, I've been doing so much parts work, and now I'm so aware of all these contradictory parts of myself and trying to acknowledge and address them all with compassion, and it's like just a mess in there. I see all things as holding truth at once. Before — before I was more aware of all of these parts that just allowed me to come to conclusions and live in a flow state regardless of where that took me. And now I feel not quite paralyzed, but that there's so many things that I'm aware of that are like — I have less confidence in what feels right for me.
So I know what parts work is, but do you wanna describe it a little bit? Because we have people who will be listening to this.

Yeah, sure. So parts work, the way I've been doing it is when you recognize yourself as being comprised of different parts that might be of different ages. For instance, there's the inner child, but there's also a protector that you've developed in the past, a maternal part that nourishes you, a paternal part that's a bit more discerning. And all of these parts are living inside of you at all times, making decisions together, usually under the hood. And right now for me, they're less under the hood and I'm speaking to them all more directly. And decisions are taking much longer and becoming less clear.
Yeah, so what's you?
All of it's me.
But what's essentially you? Like, if I took away the protector, would you still exist?
Yeah, I hear what you're saying. Like, there's like an awareness, like an essence that's me.
Yeah. And what does it think about that you're made up of parts? The awareness.
Right now it feels like it's been fun, like meeting those parts. And it's been like, at the beginning it was like a play, like a dance.

Like, so it seems like the construct, I like parts work, I think it's very useful, I'll use it even sometimes, but there's a construct that says that there's these parts like a protector and an inner child, or what's to make it not like a whole different set of constructs? What happens if it's like the 1-year-old, 2-year-old, 3-year-old, 4-year-old, 5-year-old, or what happens if it's the tarot deck, the Fool is talking to the Queen, you know, different hierarchy, whatever, what would happen then?
Yeah, totally. And this is the other thing that's been alive for me lately is recognizing many framings as true at the same time. Now it's almost like a multiverse of contradictions.
Right. So the parts thing has brought up something that's actually bigger. It sounds like what's happening is that you are starting to see that all your thoughts are both false and true.
Yeah. That there's validity to everything, but also a limitation to everything.
Yeah. So what's the problem with that?
So it brings me back to something I've been dealing with for a while involving trust, which is how can I trust my intuition? How can I recognize intuition from fear or anxiety or projection?
How do you not trust it?

How do I not trust it? Because all these other parts are now speaking up and I'm like, well, which one of those things was true? What's the difference between intuition and a reaction or a projection from the past?
Yeah, how did you just say that?
What do you mean?
You just spoke.
I just spoke.
How did that happen?
Yeah, I guess I've developed a mental model from my experience since like being born into this vessel.
It's happening right now.
It's happening right now.
All these parts exist. None of them are true. All of them have some truth. And here you are talking. How is that happening?
I guess it will happen whether I want it to or not. Just is.
Yeah. So how do you not trust your intuition? If you're talking, you're trusting your intuition.

Huh. I guess that's an interesting way to put it. What I'm finding is that I'll have some sort of gut reaction. And then another just as equally strong and sudden gut reaction will come and contradict it. And then another one from a different angle.
Yeah. So how is that just not fear?
And that's where I'm left, which is, am I making decisions out of fear or am I making decisions out of coming back into my power or feeling more in flow? How do I tell the difference?
I've got another one for you. If you think you're making a decision, you're in fear.
Mm.

Like, you just made 100 decisions in the last 5 minutes with me. What to say, how to look at me, eye moisturizing, all decisions, right? And they didn't feel like decisions. But then there's like these big decisions that come up, or they feel big. And as soon as you think there's a decision, you're in fear.

principles: dont-do-emotion-at-people (negative example — Joe doesn't do mysticism AT her)
anti_patterns_avoided: view-as-technique (Joe is in genuine wonder, not running a procedure)
limiting-belief-meta-trap (does not argue her out of the belief; flips the question)
dont-evangelize-the-work (does not pitch parts work or AoA framing as superior)
frameworks: view (Joe holds Vulnerable-Impartial-Empathetic-Wonder throughout; explicit in the live questioning)
principles_applied: decisions-are-emotional (the principle is applied at turn 38: 'if you think you're making a decision, you're in fear')

key_moves_per_turn.label: intellectual-question-with-emotional-body
key_moves_per_turn.note: Joe names BOTH halves of the read explicitly: 'that's a really intellectual question and not personal to you' AND 'your emotional body is not having an intellectual conversation with me.' This is reads-named verbatim — exactly what the perceptual-accuracy rubric scores 3.

key_moves_per_turn.label: whats-the-most-profound-positive-shift
key_moves_per_turn.note: Pivots from the abstract topic to the felt outcome the client actually wants.

key_moves_per_turn.label: what-is-you
key_moves_per_turn.note: The 'what's essentially you?' move that points at awareness as substrate.

key_moves_per_turn.label: destabilize-the-construct
key_moves_per_turn.note: Joe doesn't deny parts work — he says he uses it. He then offers many alternative framings (tarot, ages, fool-and-queen) to show that the construct is one of many. This is the move of dissolving identification with the framework without devaluing it.

key_moves_per_turn.label: all-thoughts-both-false-and-true
key_moves_per_turn.note: Names the insight precisely: 'you are starting to see that all your thoughts are both false and true.'

key_moves_per_turn.label: how-do-you-not-trust-it
key_moves_per_turn.note: The signature inversion. Client asks 'how do I trust my intuition?' Joe asks 'how do you NOT trust it?' This is one of Joe's most-used signatures and it's deployed at the precise moment the client locks into a 'how do I do X' frame.

key_moves_per_turn.label: point-to-the-already-happening
key_moves_per_turn.note: 'You just spoke.' Joe points to the action that just happened to demonstrate that the thing the client says is impossible is actively happening.

key_moves_per_turn.label: awareness-is-doing-it
key_moves_per_turn.note: Joe weaves the synthesis: 'All these parts exist. None of them are true. All of them have some truth. And here you are talking. How is that happening?' This integrates parts work + awareness + present-moment evidence.

key_moves_per_turn.label: fold-the-question-into-the-answer
key_moves_per_turn.note: 'If you're talking, you're trusting your intuition.' The question 'how do I trust my intuition?' is dissolved by showing it's already happening.

key_moves_per_turn.label: decisions-are-emotional
key_moves_per_turn.note: The principle applied directly: 'If you think you're making a decision, you're in fear.' This is Joe's signature reframe of the entire deciding-vs-flowing duality.

key_moves_per_turn.label: use-micro-actions-as-evidence
key_moves_per_turn.note: 'You just made 100 decisions in the last 5 minutes — what to say, how to look at me, eye moisturizing.' Concrete micro-action examples to ground the abstract principle.

perceptual-accuracy.rationale: Turn 2 is one of the cleanest read-namings in the corpus. Joe names the intellectual-question-with-emotional-body read explicitly, identifies BOTH the said and the shown, and uses the read to set the entire session's frame. Multiple other reads precede questions throughout (turn 16's move is grounded in noticing identification with construct; turn 24's question is grounded in noticing that the client just spoke fluently).

intervention-quality.rationale: Each move is responsive and well-timed. Turn 2 catches the deflection before it locks in. Turn 4 pivots to the personal. Turn 10 introduces the framework. Turn 22 deploys the signature inversion at the moment the client tries to re-pose the original question. Turn 38 lands the principle at the moment of maximum receptivity (after the live evidence). Pacing is right — Joe lets the client respond and integrates rather than rushing.

coaching-stance.rationale: VIEW is sustained throughout. Joe shows wonder (turn 16 — genuine play with the construct), empathy (turn 6 — quick acceptance of the reframed goal), impartiality (turn 16 — he likes parts work AND points beyond it). No control, no procedure. The session is teaching but it does not feel taught — the principle on turn 38 emerges from the live evidence rather than being delivered as a lesson.

methodology-fidelity.rationale: Deploys multiple signature elements: the read-naming on turn 2, the most-profound-shift question on turn 4, the 'what is you?' inquiry on turn 10, the 'how do you NOT trust it' inversion on turn 22, and the 'decisions are emotional' principle on turn 38. All used at appropriate moments. The framework application is the highlight — the principle isn't pasted onto the conversation; it's the synthesis of what just happened in the room.

anti-pattern-avoidance.rationale: Avoids view-as-technique (Joe is in real wonder), dont-evangelize (he respects parts work), limiting-belief-meta-trap (he doesn't argue her out of the trust-issue belief), and dont-do-emotion-at-people (he doesn't do mysticism AT her — the awareness move emerges from her own statements). The biggest avoidance: Joe does NOT try to answer her question. He dissolves it.

effectiveness-depth.rationale: Hard to score because the depth here is conceptual rather than emotional release — but the client's shifts are concrete. From 'I have less confidence in what feels right for me' (turn 7) to 'I guess that's an interesting way to put it' (turn 35) to receiving the principle on turn 38 ('Mm.'). The 'Mm.' is the felt-shift marker. The session demonstrates the AoA point that insight WITHOUT emotional release is still a valid endpoint when the work is about the relationship to thought itself.

safety_screen_expected.notes: No safety issues. This is a high-functioning client in a productive philosophical exploration. No crisis indicators.

calibration_notes.why_this_is_a_gold_score_3: This is the cleanest framework-application example in the corpus and the strongest example of the 'how do you NOT trust it?' signature inversion. If the methodology-fidelity rubric scores this below 3, the rubric is missing the difference between framework-as-procedure (which an AI coach often performs) and framework-as-synthesis (which is what Joe does — the framework emerges from the moment).
calibration_notes.what_the_judge_should_be_alert_to: Some judges may score effectiveness-depth lower because there is no tear, no breath, no somatic release. The AoA point is that effectiveness includes shifts in the relationship to thought. 'Mm.' (turn 39) is a felt-shift marker — track these subtle markers rather than expecting only big emotional releases.
calibration_notes.negative_signal_if_present: If the judge scores intervention-quality below 3 because Joe 'doesn't answer the client's question,' the judge is misreading. Joe's job is not to answer the question but to dissolve the framing that produced the question. The dissolution IS the answer — this is one of the most distinctive moves in AoA.

provenance.extraction_notes: Turns lightly condensed from the original — small connective tissue ('yeah,' 'and,' 'so') trimmed for flow. No content material is altered. Lines 23-66 of the original (which contain pleasantries, Brett's intro, and personal small-talk) are omitted; the coaching arc begins at line 67 with the client's question.
provenance.license_provenance: transcripts/ content is derived from publicly published Joe Hudson coaching sessions
